docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
MySQL is a relational database management system used to store, organize, and manage data. It allows users to create databases, store information in tables, and run queries to retrieve or modify data.

This documentation will walk you through how to use MySQL Workbench on Windows to do basic database work with its GUI (graphical interface). You'll learn to create a connection, create a schema (database), create a table, run queries, and view or edit data.

This is a beginner's guide to Workbench’s GUI, not a detailed guide to database theory or advanced MySQL.

docs.title: Intended Users
docs.text:
This documentation is designed for people who want to learn how to use Workbench, and is specifically written for people running the Windows OS. Intended readers are:

  • Beginner developers who want to learn MySQL Workbench.
  • Students learning database management and core MySQL functionalities.
  • First-time users who have never worked with a database GUI before.

docs.title: Prerequisite Knowledge
docs.text: You should be familiar with basic computer usage before following this guide. This includes opening and navigating applications, and using a mouse and keyboard to interact with softwares. You should also be comfortable navigating files and folders.

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
• MySQL Server installed and running locally, with a configured MySQL user account (for example, root) and password
  • MySQL Workbench

docs.title: Typographical Conventions
docs.text: Convention Explanation Examples Commands & actions Bolded words for commands or actions you need to perform. Click, Enter, Type, Return, Open, Select Code, SQL, and input values Monospace text for SQL commands, and input values. SELECT * FROM users;, username=root Important terms Important terms are underlined when they are first introduced. Hovering over them shows their definition. schema, table, query

docs.title: Notes, Warnings, and Results
docs.text:
You'll see branded callouts throughout the guide to highlight important information.

Common issue and fix

Steps marked Issue tell you a common issue, and how to fix it.

Warning

Steps marked Warning explain prerequisites or important conditions that may prevent you from completing the tutorial successfully.

Note

Note boxes provide extra explanation about what MySQL or Workbench is doing and why a step matters.

Success

Success shows the expected result so you can confirm you’re on the right track.

docs.title: 1. Create a connection
docs.text: Before creating schemas, tables, or queries in Workbench, you need to connect to an SQL Server with a connection. This part shows how to create a new connection in Workbench.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open MySQL Workbench.

  2. Left-click the + icon next to MySQL Connections on the home screen.

  3. Type the following details into the Setup New Connection window:

    • A name (of your choosing) in Connection Name
    • localhost in Hostname
    • 3306 in Port
    • root in Username

    Common issue and fix

    If root does not work for username, check the username you created when you installed MySQL Server and use that instead of root. The username is usually root by default unless you set a different username during setup.

  4. Left-click the Test Connection button to verify the connection is working. If prompted, enter your MySQL password.

    Success

    If the connection settings are correct, Workbench will display a successful connection message:

    Common issue and fix

    If the connection test fails, double-check your password, hostname, and port number (3306) to make sure you have no typos. Most connection issues are caused by incorrect login details.

  5. Left-click OK to save the connection.

    Warning

    Before continuing to the next part, you should left-click on your connection (the box highlighted in red) to open it.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You've created a connection in MySQL Workbench. You can use this to access your MySQL server to do things like creating a schema, creating tables, and running queries.

docs.title: 2. Create a Schema
docs.text:
A schema in Workbench represents a database that stores tables and data in those tables. This section walks you through how to create a new schema.

Warning

Make sure you already created a connection and it's opened right now. Refer to Create a connection.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Locate the schema panel on the left side of the Workbench menu.

  2. Left-click the Create Schema button highlighted in red.

  3. Type a name for your schema in the Name box.

  4. Left-click the Apply button.

  5. Read the confirmation window showing the SQL command that will be executed.

  6. Left-click the Apply button.

    Success

    If the SQL command runs correctly, you'll see this:

  7. Left-click the Finish button.

  8. Left-click the Refresh button circled in red if you don't see your new schema in the schema panel.

    Success

    Your newly created schema should show up in the schemas panel on the left side.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You've created a schema in MySQL Workbench. You'll use this schema to create a table in the next part.

docs.title: 3. Create a table
docs.text:
This section walks you through how to create a table using Workbench.

Warning

Before making a table, make sure you've already created a schema. Refer to Create a schema.

You should be familiar with the contents of the previous sections so you can follow this part easily!

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Locate the schema panel on the left side of the Workbench menu.

  2. Left-click the button highlighted in red to expand your schema.

  3. Right-click the Tables section under your schema.

  4. Left-click the Create Table option.

  5. Type a name for your table in the name box.

  6. Enter each column’s details in the row of the Columns section.

    Example:

    For each column:

    • Type a name in Column Name. We called it student_id but you can choose your own name.
    • Select a value in Datatype. For this tutorial, please select the INT datatype, but you can choose other datatypes later.
    • Left-click to check any options you want to apply, like PK for primary key, NN for not null, or AI for auto increment.

    Common issue and fix

    If you can't find the Column editing section, left-click the button highlighted in red to open it:

  7. Left-click the Apply button only when you are finished naming the table, and putting in column details.

  8. Read the confirmation window showing the SQL command that will be executed.

  9. Left-click the Apply button.

    Success

    If the SQL command runs correctly, you'll see this:

    Common issue and fix

    If the SQL script doesn't run correctly and you have an error, double-check the details you entered on the table creation window to make sure you have no typos.

  10. Left-click the Finish button.

  11. Left-click the button highlighted in red to expand the Tables section to confirm your table exists. If the table does not show up, left-click the Refresh button.

    Success

    You successfully created a new table in your schema.

    Note

    It's recommended to create a Primary Key (PK) for your table, using an INT column with Auto Increment (AI). This ensures each row has a unique identifier.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You've created a table in Workbench. You'll use this table to insert data in the next part.

docs.title: 4. Insert data
docs.text:
In MySQL Workbench, data is stored inside tables as rows. To add new information, users insert data into the columns of a table. This section explains how to insert data using the MySQL Workbench graphical interface.

Warning

Before inserting data, make sure you have already created a table. Refer to Create a table.

You should be familiar with the contents of the previous sections so you can follow this part easily!

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Locate the schema panel on the left side of the Workbench menu.

  2. Expand your schema and locate the table you want to insert data into.

  3. Right-click the table, and left-click the Select Rows option.

    Success

    You should see the Result Grid appear after clicking Select Rows.

  4. Left-click on an empty column to be able to type in it.

  5. Type values into each column.

    • You can type directly into each cell.

    • Follow the datatype requirements (e.g. numbers for INT, text for VARCHAR).

  6. Left-click the Apply button in the bottom right corner.

    • If you accidentally entered incorrect data, you can click the Revert button beside Apply to erase what you just put.

  7. Read the confirmation window showing the SQL command that will be executed.

  8. Left-click the Apply button.

    Success

    If the SQL command runs correctly, you'll see this:

    Common issue and fix

    • Make sure your required columns ( marked with NN for Not Null ) are filled in before clicking the Apply button, or the insert will fail.

    • If you used the wrong datatype in a column, it will fail. For example, typing text like hello into an INT column that requires and integer like 1, 2, 3 will fail.

  9. Left-click the Refresh button if you do not see changes in the Result Grid.

    Success

    You successfully inserted data into a table, you'll the data here:

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Now your table contains saved rows, and you can now view, edit, or query that data. We'll query that data in the next part.

docs.title: 5. Running queries
docs.text:
A query is an SQL command used to retrieve or work with data. In MySQL Workbench, queries are written and run in the SQL Editor. This part explains how to run a query using Workbench.

Warning

Before following this part, make sure you've already inserted data into a table, and you have your connection opened. Refer to Inserting data into tables.

You should be familiar with the contents of the previous sections so you can follow this part easily!

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Locate the schema panel on the left side of the Workbench menu.

  2. Expand your schema, then expand the Tables section to confirm the table you created exists.

  3. Left-click File at the top left, then left-click New Query Tab to create a new query tab in the SQL Editor.

    Note

    You can also press Ctrl + T at the same time on your keyboard to create a tab.

    The area highlighted in red is your SQL Editor. This is where you write and run queries:

  4. Type this into the SQL Editor:

    USE name_of_your_schema;
SELECT * FROM name_of_your_table;

    Replace the placeholder names with the actual names of your schema and table.

    Note

    This part uses a USE and SELECT query because it's safe for beginners.

  5. Left-click the Execute button (lightning bolt icon) to run the query.

    Success

    If the query runs successfully, you'll see the data from your table in the Result Grid, as well as an Output showing the query that was executed.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You just ran a query in Workbench. You can use the SQL Editor to write and run your SQL scripts.

docs.title: 6. View and edit data
docs.text:
In Workbench you can view and change data in a table, allowing you to update values, correct mistakes, and more. This part walks you through how to view and edit table data in Workbench.

Warning

Before following this part, make sure you've already inserted data into a table, and you have your connection opened. Refer to Inserting data into tables.

You should be familiar with the contents of the previous sections so you can follow this part easily!

docs.title: Editing data
docs.text:
1. Locate the schema panel on the left side of the Workbench menu.

  2. Expand your schema and locate the table you want to view or edit.

  3. Right-click the table you want to edit, and left-click the Select Rows option. The Result Grid from the last part will show up again.

  4. Left-click on the cell you want to edit and type the new value.

  5. Apply the changes you made to the cell the same way you did in the previous parts.

  6. Refresh the Result Grid, your table should be edited now.

docs.title: Deleting data
docs.text:
1. Left-click on the button highlighted in red beside the row you want to delete, and then right-click on the same button to open the options menu for that row.

  2. Left-click the Delete Row(s) option in the options menu.

    Warning

    Be careful when editing values in a Primary Key column. Primary Keys must remain unique, so changing them may cause errors or affect how rows are identified.

  3. Apply the changes the same way you did in the previous parts.

  4. Refresh the Result Grid, your row should be deleted now.

    Success

    The row you deleted should be empty now.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You just viewed and edited table data in Workbench. This way you can update existing values and remove unwanted rows.
